frames.caption: Init slow and fast both at the head (node 1). slow will crawl, fast will sprint two-for-one.
frames.note: Two pointers from the same start, different speeds. The "tortoise and hare" idea: let one pointer outrun the other at exactly double speed and geometry does the rest — when the hare finishes, the tortoise is precisely halfway. This same fast/slow skeleton also powers cycle detection (LC 141) and finding the nth-from-end.

frames.caption: Check loop: fast = 1 (not null) and fast.next = 2 (not null) → keep going.
frames.note: The trick is a speed difference, not a length precount. fast covers twice the distance of slow, so by the time fast has walked the whole list, slow has walked exactly half — landing on the middle. The guard is two-pronged: `fast != null` handles even lengths (fast steps cleanly off the end into null), `fast.next != null` handles odd lengths (fast stops on the last node). One pass, O(n) time, O(1) space.

frames.caption: slow advances one node → slow = 2.
frames.note: Each iteration moves slow one node and fast two nodes. Think of fast as a scout running ahead at double speed: the gap between them grows by one node every step, so when fast reaches the end the gap IS half the list — putting slow at the midpoint. No counter, no second traversal.

frames.caption: fast advances two nodes → fast = 3.
frames.note: Each iteration moves slow one node and fast two nodes. Think of fast as a scout running ahead at double speed: the gap between them grows by one node every step, so when fast reaches the end the gap IS half the list — putting slow at the midpoint. No counter, no second traversal.

frames.caption: Check loop: fast = 3 (not null) and fast.next = 4 (not null) → keep going.
frames.note: The trick is a speed difference, not a length precount. fast covers twice the distance of slow, so by the time fast has walked the whole list, slow has walked exactly half — landing on the middle. The guard is two-pronged: `fast != null` handles even lengths (fast steps cleanly off the end into null), `fast.next != null` handles odd lengths (fast stops on the last node). One pass, O(n) time, O(1) space.

frames.caption: slow advances one node → slow = 3.
frames.note: Each iteration moves slow one node and fast two nodes. Think of fast as a scout running ahead at double speed: the gap between them grows by one node every step, so when fast reaches the end the gap IS half the list — putting slow at the midpoint. No counter, no second traversal.

frames.caption: fast advances two nodes → fast = 5.
frames.note: Each iteration moves slow one node and fast two nodes. Think of fast as a scout running ahead at double speed: the gap between them grows by one node every step, so when fast reaches the end the gap IS half the list — putting slow at the midpoint. No counter, no second traversal.

frames.caption: Loop guard fails (fast.next == null) — fast has reached the end. slow is now at the middle.
frames.note: For an EVEN length the standard loop returns the SECOND of the two middles (LC 876 convention): e.g. for 1→2→3→4 fast becomes null after slow reaches node 3, so it returns 3, not 2. To get the FIRST middle instead (what Reorder List needs), start fast = head.next or stop on fast.next.next == null.

frames.caption: Return slow = node 3 — the middle of 1→2→3→4→5.
frames.note: Because fast traveled exactly twice slow's distance, slow stopped at the halfway point in a single pass — no length count, no extra storage. For an odd length this is the unique center; for an even length it is the upper (second) of the two centers per LC 876. O(n) time, O(1) space.
